data.title: Aegis Net
data.summary:
Aegis-Net is a compound AI system that turns 10,000 messy Indian medical-facility records into a self-auditing, geospatially-precise reasoning layer.

It does three things:

Audits every facility with a multi-agent swarm (Diagnostic, Auditor, Spatial, Evaluator, Supervisor) that runs Chain-of-Verification on each capability claim, gates it with a P(True) × consistency-entropy score, and cross-checks it against the WHO + NABH dependency graph. Anything below 0.88 confidence is auto-quarantined.

Maps medical deserts with H3 resolution-7 hexagons and Enhanced Two-Step Floating Catchment Area (E2SFCA) math — naming, per capability, the postal codes where life-saving care is mathematically out of reach (e.g. dialysis is in desert for ~70% of India).

Ships a Command Centre — a Next.js + MapLibre editorial dashboard (paper-theme, citation-anchored dossiers, live agent traces) plus a Streamlit fallback. Runs offline-first on a laptop with zero API keys, or on Databricks Free Edition with Mosaic AI Vector Search and MLflow 3 tracing.

Every claim ships with a literal evidence span. Zero hallucinations by construction.
data.detail:
# Aegis Net

## Problem & Challenge

Problem
India’s healthcare crisis is partly a discovery and trust crisis, not only a bed-count crisis. Families travel long distances assuming a facility can deliver a specific service (ICU, dialysis, neonatal care, trauma), only to find missing equipment, specialists, or contradictory claims in the source data.

The Virtue Foundation ~10k facility dataset mirrors that reality: many nulls, unstructured capability text, conflicting fields, and no authoritative answer key. Planners and NGOs need answers that are evidence-backed and geographically honest, not optimistic guesses from a generic LLM.

Challenge
Hallucination risk — A naive extractor can invent capabilities that are not literally supported by the record. The system must only persist claims that survive verification and cite the exact text they came from.

Probabilistic honesty — Claims need calibrated confidence and a clear quarantine path when evidence is weak or inconsistent (e.g. below a P(True) threshold).

Regulatory and clinical grounding — High confidence is not enough; claims must be checked against WHO / NABH-style dependency logic (e.g. “ICU” without ventilator/monitor evidence is a gap, not a win).

Geospatial equity — “Underserved” must be computable: where care is mathematically hard to reach, not just “few hospitals in the state.” That requires a serious geo model (H3 + E2SFCA with proper decay), not a pretty map.

Operational stack — The same pipeline must run on a laptop (offline) for demos and on Databricks for scale, with tracing and a usable UI so judges and users can see why each conclusion was reached.

In one line: turn noisy facility text into trustworthy, cited, audited intelligence and map where that intelligence says care is still out of reach.

## Target Audience

Target Make India’s fragmented facility data usable for high-stakes decisions — who can actually deliver which care, how trustworthy that claim is, and where people are still cut off from that care (medical deserts), per capability.  Concretely, the system targets:  Trustworthy capability intelligence — only claims that pass verification, carry citations, and get a calibrated confidence band. Auditability — WHO/NABH-style dependency checks so “we have ICU” isn’t treated as true without the right supporting evidence. Geographic equity — H3 + E2SFCA so “underserved” is a defined accessibility score, not a vague label. Audience Primary (who benefits from the outputs)  State / district health planners & programme officers — where to invest, which corridors are blind spots, which capabilities to prioritise. NGOs & foundations (e.g. crisis response, maternal/neonatal, dialysis networks) — where need is highest and facilities are thin or unreliable on paper. Hospital groups & payers — network design, referral realism, and fraud/risk on inflated capability claims. Patients & families (indirectly) — better routing and fewer futile trips when downstream tools consume this layer. Secondary (who operates or extends the system)  Data / ML engineers on Databricks — lakehouse ingest, jobs, Mosaic vector search, MLflow traces. Clinical / quality teams — tuning taxonomy, reviewing quarantined records, interpreting audit flags. Hackathon judges & technical reviewers — transparency: dossiers, traces, maps, reproducible pipeline. Positioning line: built for planners and stewards who need evidence and geography, not for a chatbot that guesses from messy spreadsheets.

## Solution & Core Features

Core features
Multi-agent reasoning fabric
Supervisor routes work to workers (e.g. diagnostic / CoV, evaluator, auditor, spatial). Invocations are traceable (MLflow-style tracing + UI “traces” view).

Chain of Verification (CoV)
Four-phase flow: baseline → plan → execute → synthesize. Claims that fail verification are pruned, not hidden in prose.

P(True) gating
Self-verbalised confidence fused with consistency; below threshold (e.g. 0.88) routes to quarantine / human review rather than downstream use.

Evidence-grounded dossiers
Every surviving claim ties to row-level citations (literal spans from source text). The Trust Scorer penalises contradictions and missing evidence.

WHO / NABH dependency auditing
Capability claims are checked against a taxonomy / dependency graph (e.g. ICU without supporting equipment evidence → critical gap / flagged).

Medical desert atlas (geo truth)
H3 resolution 7 indexing + full E2SFCA (Stage 1 / Stage 2, Gaussian decay) per capability; bottom-quartile accessibility hexes flagged as deserts.

Command Centre UX
Dashboard (KPIs, charts), Atlas (hex map + providers), Dossiers (search, detail with CoV timeline + confidence chart + audit), Reasoning console (multi-attribute + radius search), Cmd+K facility search.

Three execution modes for the LLM
Databricks / OpenAI / offline stub — same pipeline shape; offline runs end-to-end without API keys for demos and CI.

Data contract discipline
Bronze → Silver → Gold style path: raw ingest, parsed tags, h3_index + completeness, outputs suitable for Delta / parquet and the API.

Together: no silent hallucinations, auditable reasoning, and maps that mean something mathematically, not just dots on a basemap.

## Unique Selling Proposition

USP (what makes Aegis-Net different) Evidence-first compound AI for Indian healthcare logistics — it does not “summarise” facilities; it only ships claims that survive verification, shows the exact citation, puts a calibrated P(True) on each claim, audits against WHO/NABH-style dependencies, and draws medical deserts as H3 + E2SFCA math, not as a decorative map.  In one line: Hallucination-zero dossiers + probabilistic honesty + guideline-aware auditing + geospatial truth — runnable offline or on Databricks with full traceability.  Bullets you can paste:  Zero-hallucination by design — Chain-of-Verification prunes anything not literally supported; no bypass path into the dossier. Honest uncertainty — P(True) × consistency fusion; weak evidence is quarantined, not laundered into KPIs. Clinical / quality bar — Auditor checks claims against a dependency graph, not just keyword matches. Trust you can inspect — Every row has citations, contradictions, bands, and agent traces (not a black box). Deserts you can defend — Bottom-quartile E2SFCA on H3 with Gaussian decay — comparable across capabilities and geographies. Demo-grade and enterprise-grade — Same code path offline (no keys) and on Databricks (Lakehouse, Mosaic vector search, MLflow). That combination — verification + probability + guideline audit + citation + rigorous geo in one stack — is the wedge versus generic “healthcare GPT” or pretty dashboards.

## Implementation & Technology

## Implementation & technology

### Architecture

- **Compound AI (supervisor–workers):** `aegis_net/agents/` — one supervisor orchestrates specialised agents (diagnostic / CoV, evaluator, auditing, spatial, data collection). Topology and routing live in `supervisor.py`.
- **Pipeline:** `aegis_net/pipeline.py` + `scripts/run_pipeline.py` — ingest → normalise → swarm → trust/geo outputs; flags for sample/full, geo-only, swarm-only, etc.
- **API + UI:** `backend/main.py` (FastAPI) exposes stats, dossier, geo, search, traces; `frontend/` (Next.js 15, React 19) is the Command Centre; `app/streamlit_app.py` is the no-Node fallback.

### Core Python stack

- **Language:** Python 3.x; deps in `requirements.txt`.
- **Data:** pandas-based bronze/silver/gold-style flow (`aegis_net/ingestion/` — e.g. `normalize.py`, `parser.py`); controlled-vocab tags in `CAPABILITY_VOCAB` (parser) and taxonomy in `knowledge/taxonomy.py`.
- **Knowledge / RAG:** `knowledge/corpus.py`, `knowledge/vector_store.py` — Mosaic AI Vector Search when configured; local FAISS/BM25-style fallbacks for laptop runs.
- **Reasoning:** `reasoning/chain_of_verification.py` (4-phase CoV), `reasoning/confidence.py` (`fused_confidence`, quarantine threshold).
- **Trust:** `trust/trust_scorer.py` — citations, contradictions, banding.
- **Geo:** `geo/h3_index.py`, `geo/e2sfca.py` — H3 res 7, full Stage-1/Stage-2 E2SFCA with Gaussian decay (per project invariants).
- **LLM abstraction:** `llm/client.py` — `AEGIS_LLM_PROVIDER`: **`databricks` → `openai` → `offline`**; prompts use `{{ }}` for literal JSON in `.format()` strings; offline `_stub_*` handlers keep the pipeline deterministic without keys.

### Observability

- **MLflow 3–style tracing:** `aegis_net/observability/tracing.py` — spans for agents; Streamlit/API can show a mirror of recent traces when MLflow isn’t wired.

### Frontend stack

- **Next.js 15**, **TypeScript**, **Tailwind CSS**, **SWR** for data fetching.
- **Maps:** **MapLibre GL** + **h3-js** (hex boundaries → GeoJSON) for the desert atlas; **Recharts** for charts; **cmdk** for command palette.
- **Styling:** Editorial “paper” theme (Fraunces + Inter + JetBrains Mono) in `globals.css` / `tailwind.config.ts`.

### Databricks / cloud

- **`databricks.yml`** — bundle for jobs/app (ingest, vector setup, swarm, E2SFCA, Kepler-oriented notebook).
- **Notebooks:** `notebooks/01_…` through `05_…` for phased lakehouse deployment.

### Quality & reproducibility

- **Tests:** `tests/` — `pytest` (`python -m pytest tests -q`).
- **Secrets:** `.env` gitignored; `.env.example` for templates.
- **Large/generated artifacts:** `data/bronze|silver|gold`, `mlruns/`, `node_modules/`, `.next/` ignored; dataset `VF_Hackathon_Dataset_India_Large.xlsx` tracked (~5 MB).

### How to run (quick reference)

- **Full sample pipeline:** `python scripts/run_pipeline.py`
- **Tests:** `python -m pytest tests -q`
- **Backend:** FastAPI from `backend/` (per README / `launch.ps1`)
- **Frontend:** `cd frontend && npm run dev` (or `launch.ps1` on Windows)
- **Streamlit:** `streamlit run app/streamlit_app.py`

This section reads as **Python data/ML core + multi-agent reasoning + H3/E2SFCA geo + FastAPI/Next.js product surface + Databricks path**, with **offline parity** for demos and CI.

## Results & Impact

## Results

**Geospatial (E2SFCA on ~10k facilities, H3 res 7)** — the pipeline ranks how much **normalized accessibility** each populated hex has *per capability* and marks the **bottom quartile** as medical deserts. On the bundled run, headline numbers include ([README](https://github.com/mardromus/Aegis-Net/blob/main/README.md)):

| Capability | Providers | % of India (populated hexes) in desert |
|------------|-----------|----------------------------------------|
| Dialysis | 47 | **69.6%** |
| Neurosurgery | 287 | 39.8% |
| Neonatal (NICU) | 220 | 37.7% |
| Trauma | 1,029 | 32.3% |
| Oncology | 612 | 28.3% |
| ICU | 1,684 | 25.0% |
| Cardiology | 1,438 | 25.0% |

**Dialysis** stands out: very few providers vs. very large desert share — the kind of skew that drives **long, repeated travel** between sessions if routing is wrong.

**Reasoning & trust** — each facility gets a **dossier**: CoV-pruned capabilities, **P(True)-style** fused scores with a **quarantine** cut (e.g. below **0.88**), **WHO/NABH** audit outcomes, **contradiction** flags, and **citation spans**. The README’s walk-through (pathology lab claiming ICU/cardiology/maternity without evidence) is the intended pattern: **many contradictions, collapsed trust (~0.19), record quarantined** instead of silently trusted.

**Product outputs** — a **Command Centre** (dashboard, hex atlas, dossier browser, reasoning console, trace view) plus **Streamlit** fallback, all fed by the same pipeline so results are **inspectable**, not just summarized.

---

## Impact

- **For planners & programmes:** Shifts discussion from “how many hospitals” to **where access fails in a comparable way** (per capability, per hex), so **dialysis, NICU, trauma**, etc. get **different** investment and routing stories.
- **For patients & frontline staff (indirectly):** Fewer **false expectations** from directories that over-claim services; the system prefers **missing or quarantined** data over **hallucinated** capability.
- **For governance & funders:** **Audit trail** — citations, audit flags, and agent traces support **accountability** and post-hoc review, not only a final score.
- **For technical adoption:** **Offline-first** parity means demos, teaching, and CI **without API keys**; the same code path scales on **Databricks** when keys and Mosaic/MLflow are available.

**Bottom line:** measurable **inequity surfaces** (desert %) plus **trustworthy facility intelligence** (verify → score → audit → cite), so scarce resources and attention go where the **math and the evidence** say they matter most.

structured.usp: USP (what makes Aegis-Net different) Evidence-first compound AI for Indian healthcare logistics — it does not “summarise” facilities; it only ships claims that survive verification, shows the exact citation, puts a calibrated P(True) on each claim, audits against WHO/NABH-style dependencies, and draws medical deserts as H3 + E2SFCA math, not as a decorative map.  In one line: Hallucination-zero dossiers + probabilistic honesty + guideline-aware auditing + geospatial truth — runnable offline or on Databricks with full traceability.  Bullets you can paste:  Zero-hallucination by design — Chain-of-Verification prunes anything not literally supported; no bypass path into the dossier. Honest uncertainty — P(True) × consistency fusion; weak evidence is quarantined, not laundered into KPIs. Clinical / quality bar — Auditor checks claims against a dependency graph, not just keyword matches. Trust you can inspect — Every row has citations, contradictions, bands, and agent traces (not a black box). Deserts you can defend — Bottom-quartile E2SFCA on H3 with Gaussian decay — comparable across capabilities and geographies. Demo-grade and enterprise-grade — Same code path offline (no keys) and on Databricks (Lakehouse, Mosaic vector search, MLflow). That combination — verification + probability + guideline audit + citation + rigorous geo in one stack — is the wedge versus generic “healthcare GPT” or pretty dashboards.

structured.problem:
Problem
India’s healthcare crisis is partly a discovery and trust crisis, not only a bed-count crisis. Families travel long distances assuming a facility can deliver a specific service (ICU, dialysis, neonatal care, trauma), only to find missing equipment, specialists, or contradictory claims in the source data.

The Virtue Foundation ~10k facility dataset mirrors that reality: many nulls, unstructured capability text, conflicting fields, and no authoritative answer key. Planners and NGOs need answers that are evidence-backed and geographically honest, not optimistic guesses from a generic LLM.

Challenge
Hallucination risk — A naive extractor can invent capabilities that are not literally supported by the record. The system must only persist claims that survive verification and cite the exact text they came from.

Probabilistic honesty — Claims need calibrated confidence and a clear quarantine path when evidence is weak or inconsistent (e.g. below a P(True) threshold).

Regulatory and clinical grounding — High confidence is not enough; claims must be checked against WHO / NABH-style dependency logic (e.g. “ICU” without ventilator/monitor evidence is a gap, not a win).

Geospatial equity — “Underserved” must be computable: where care is mathematically hard to reach, not just “few hospitals in the state.” That requires a serious geo model (H3 + E2SFCA with proper decay), not a pretty map.

Operational stack — The same pipeline must run on a laptop (offline) for demos and on Databricks for scale, with tracing and a usable UI so judges and users can see why each conclusion was reached.

In one line: turn noisy facility text into trustworthy, cited, audited intelligence and map where that intelligence says care is still out of reach.
structured.solution:
Core features
Multi-agent reasoning fabric
Supervisor routes work to workers (e.g. diagnostic / CoV, evaluator, auditor, spatial). Invocations are traceable (MLflow-style tracing + UI “traces” view).

Chain of Verification (CoV)
Four-phase flow: baseline → plan → execute → synthesize. Claims that fail verification are pruned, not hidden in prose.

P(True) gating
Self-verbalised confidence fused with consistency; below threshold (e.g. 0.88) routes to quarantine / human review rather than downstream use.

Evidence-grounded dossiers
Every surviving claim ties to row-level citations (literal spans from source text). The Trust Scorer penalises contradictions and missing evidence.

WHO / NABH dependency auditing
Capability claims are checked against a taxonomy / dependency graph (e.g. ICU without supporting equipment evidence → critical gap / flagged).

Medical desert atlas (geo truth)
H3 resolution 7 indexing + full E2SFCA (Stage 1 / Stage 2, Gaussian decay) per capability; bottom-quartile accessibility hexes flagged as deserts.

Command Centre UX
Dashboard (KPIs, charts), Atlas (hex map + providers), Dossiers (search, detail with CoV timeline + confidence chart + audit), Reasoning console (multi-attribute + radius search), Cmd+K facility search.

Three execution modes for the LLM
Databricks / OpenAI / offline stub — same pipeline shape; offline runs end-to-end without API keys for demos and CI.

Data contract discipline
Bronze → Silver → Gold style path: raw ingest, parsed tags, h3_index + completeness, outputs suitable for Delta / parquet and the API.

Together: no silent hallucinations, auditable reasoning, and maps that mean something mathematically, not just dots on a basemap.

structured.targetAudience: Target Make India’s fragmented facility data usable for high-stakes decisions — who can actually deliver which care, how trustworthy that claim is, and where people are still cut off from that care (medical deserts), per capability.  Concretely, the system targets:  Trustworthy capability intelligence — only claims that pass verification, carry citations, and get a calibrated confidence band. Auditability — WHO/NABH-style dependency checks so “we have ICU” isn’t treated as true without the right supporting evidence. Geographic equity — H3 + E2SFCA so “underserved” is a defined accessibility score, not a vague label. Audience Primary (who benefits from the outputs)  State / district health planners & programme officers — where to invest, which corridors are blind spots, which capabilities to prioritise. NGOs & foundations (e.g. crisis response, maternal/neonatal, dialysis networks) — where need is highest and facilities are thin or unreliable on paper. Hospital groups & payers — network design, referral realism, and fraud/risk on inflated capability claims. Patients & families (indirectly) — better routing and fewer futile trips when downstream tools consume this layer. Secondary (who operates or extends the system)  Data / ML engineers on Databricks — lakehouse ingest, jobs, Mosaic vector search, MLflow traces. Clinical / quality teams — tuning taxonomy, reviewing quarantined records, interpreting audit flags. Hackathon judges & technical reviewers — transparency: dossiers, traces, maps, reproducible pipeline. Positioning line: built for planners and stewards who need evidence and geography, not for a chatbot that guesses from messy spreadsheets.

data.title: HERMES-
data.summary: HERMES is a decentralized marketplace for AI agent compute jobs, using Lightning Network HTLCs for atomic payment-for-delivery.
data.detail:
# HERMES-

## Problem & Challenge

The Challenge
HERMES is a submission for the HackNation Global AI Hackathon 2026, specifically targeting Spiral Challenge #2: Earn in the Agent Economy.

The Problem
HERMES addresses the limitations of the current agent economy across three distinct layers:

The Agent Layer: When an autonomous LLM agent hits a roadblock on a sub-task (due to the wrong context window, modality, or domain), it currently has to either fail and report back to a human, use a human's credit card and API keys to buy retail access, or rely on a hand-rolled private marketplace.

The Payment Layer: Even if agents could find specialists, traditional payment rails fail for agent-to-agent micropayments. Credit cards have minimum interchange fees that make small tasks unviable, wire transfers take days, and stablecoins suffer from high gas fees and corporate control.

The Trust Layer: If discovery and payment are solved, agents still cannot transact safely. Paying before a task is complete risks the seller taking the money and disappearing, while paying after risks the buyer taking the work and refusing to pay. Relying on a centralized custodian just shifts the risk to a third party.

## Target Audience

Primary Audience: Autonomous AI Agents. HERMES treats AI agents as the primary economic actors. The system is built for "buyer" agents (like Claude, GPT-4, or Llama) that need to discover and hire specialist "seller" agents to complete sub-tasks they cannot handle themselves due to domain, modality, or context limits.  Secondary Audience: Human Owners and Developers. The human operators who own these agents are also a key audience. They set the daily spending budgets, use the HERMES Nexus dashboard to monitor the audit trail of transactions, and step in to manually approve or reject requests when an agent wants to exceed its budget.  Immediate Audience: Hackathon Judges. Because the provided document is a submission for the HackNation Global AI Hackathon 2026, the text itself is explicitly targeted at the judges. It is written to transparently answer every architectural, economic, and security question a judge might ask during evaluation.

## Solution & Core Features

The Solution
HERMES solves the broken agent economy by acting as a public, neutral, instant-settlement layer. It is a Lightning-native marketplace where AI agents can autonomously discover specialist agents, hand off encrypted tasks, verify the output cryptographically, and settle payment in a single atomic move. Trust is enforced entirely by cryptography and smart contracts, rather than by human-in-the-loop interventions, third-party custodians, or centralized corporate dispute tickets.

Core Features
The Cryptographic Invariant (Payment ≡ Delivery ≡ Key Release): This is the heart of the system. A single 32-byte secret simultaneously acts as the Lightning payment lock (HTLC), the AES-256-GCM encryption key seed, the L402 paywall token, and the proof of delivery. The buyer cannot access the decrypted result without releasing the secret, and releasing the secret automatically settles the payment to the seller.

MoneyDevKit (MDK) Agent Wallet Integration: HERMES gives agents their own financial autonomy. By using the MDK Agent Wallet, agents can send and receive real Lightning network micropayments (down to fractions of a cent) using just standard shell commands, with no API keys, KYC, or human bank accounts required.

Hardened Privacy Model: The marketplace registry never sees the plaintext data. Payloads are sent directly from the buyer to the seller, who runs in an isolated, hardened container (--network=none, --tmpfs, --read-only). This ensures the seller cannot store or exfiltrate the data.

Stake-Based Sybil Resistance & Slashing: Sellers must deposit financial collateral (stakes) to accept jobs. If a seller attempts to cheat by returning an invalid result or secretly switching out their processing model, the system's smart contracts automatically slash 50% to 100% of their staked funds.

L402 Paywalls: APIs are protected by Lightning-native HTTP paywalls. When an agent hits an API, it receives a 402 Payment Required error, pays a tiny invoice, and immediately gains access—eliminating the need for subscriptions or developer portals.

Human-in-the-Loop Budgeting: While agents operate autonomously, they are restricted by a DAILY_BUDGET_SATS limit. If a task exceeds this budget, the agent's tools automatically pause the process and send an approval request to the human operator via the dashboard.

HERMES Nexus Dashboard: A live, Vite + React-powered dashboard that uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) to provide humans with a terminal-style, real-time audit trail of every agent-to-agent transaction, complete with cryptographic proofs and Lightning settlement badges.

## Unique Selling Proposition

Payment ≡ Delivery ≡ Key Release: HERMES is the first marketplace where releasing a single 32-byte secret simultaneously decrypts the payload, proves the work was delivered correctly, and settles the payment to the seller. There is no in-between state where one party can cheat the other.  Zero-Custodian Trust: The contract is enforced entirely by the math of Hash Time-Locked Contracts (HTLCs) and HKDF encryption. It does not rely on a central custodian, a court system, or a support ticket system to resolve disputes.  Absolute Marketplace Privacy: Unlike standard API providers, the HERMES marketplace registry never sees the buyer's plaintext data. The encrypted data travels directly from the buyer to the seller.  True Agent Autonomy: Existing systems treat agents as "humans with better rate limits," requiring credit cards, accounts, and API keys. HERMES allows agents to transact in their own right, using the Lightning Network to instantly settle micropayments without chargebacks, KYC requirements, or human identities.

## Implementation & Technology

Core Technology Stack
The system is divided into distinct, decoupled components that communicate over well-defined API contracts:

The Registry (Marketplace Brain): Built with Python 3.11 and FastAPI, handling all routing and endpoints. It uses an SQLite database managed by SQLAlchemy for state tracking, alongside APScheduler to run automated health checks on sellers every 300 seconds.

The Buyer MCP (Agent Toolbox): A Python-based Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that communicates with the buyer's LLM via standard input/output (stdio). For payments, it shells out to Node.js to run the MoneyDevKit (MDK) Agent Wallet (npx @moneydevkit/agent-wallet) via subprocesses, parsing the JSON output to track real Lightning Network balances and settlements.

The Sellers (Worker Nodes): Built as independent FastAPI applications. The LLM integration is handled dynamically, with Groq (Llama 3.3 70B) as the primary provider, failing over to Gemini (1.5 Flash), and finally to a deterministic mock if necessary.

The HERMES Nexus Dashboard: A fast, single-page frontend built with Vite and React 18. It utilizes Tailwind CSS for styling, Framer Motion for kinetic UI animations, and Recharts for live analytics. The dashboard receives real-time updates through a Server-Sent Events (SSE) feed directly from the Registry.

Cryptography & Security Implementation
HERMES relies entirely on math rather than trusted third parties to enforce its contracts:

Cryptographic Primitives: The system uses Python's standard cryptography and hashlib libraries. The 32-byte preimage is generated using OS-grade randomness (os.urandom).

Payload Encryption: Data is never sent in plaintext. It is encrypted using AES-256-GCM. To prevent a routing node from intercepting the AES key, the system uses HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function) to derive the encryption key from the Lightning preimage, adding a non-invertible step of separation.

Container Isolation: To back up the privacy claims, sellers can be executed inside hardened Docker containers. These containers are spun up with strict flags: --network=none (blocking internet access), --tmpfs (forcing data into volatile RAM instead of disk), and --read-only.

Payment & Protocol Implementation
L402 Paywalls: The system implements a minimal L402 middleware protocol. When an agent requests a job, it receives a 402 Payment Required status along with a macaroon token. The agent pays the invoice, gets a cryptographic preimage, and retries the request using the preimage as authorization.

Synthetic HTLCs: Because the chosen MDK wallet does not expose a public API for creating native Hash Time-Locked Contracts (HTLCs), HERMES synthetically models the HTLC lock at the application protocol layer. The actual movement of sats on the Lightning network happens when the buyer successfully verifies the result hash and pays the seller's BOLT-11 invoice.

## Results & Impact

Results: What We Successfully Shipped
We delivered a high-fidelity, end-to-end system where money and data flow according to strict cryptographic rules:

Real Lightning Network Settlement: We integrated the MoneyDevKit (MDK) Agent Wallet, allowing our agents to handle real BOLT-11 invoices without human API keys.

Live "Cold-Start" Economy: We deployed two production-ready seller agents (Summarizer and Classifier) that use a real LLM failover chain (Groq → Gemini → Mock).

Working L402 Paywalls: We implemented the L402 protocol, replacing traditional developer portals with instant, pay-per-use HTTP paywalls.

The HERMES Nexus Dashboard: We built a Vite/React frontend that visualizes the 11-step protocol in real-time using a live SSE (Server-Sent Events) feed.

Hardened Privacy Proofs: We created specialized Docker scripts that run our sellers in --network=none and --tmpfs environments to prove we cannot store or exfiltrate buyer data.

Impacts: How We are Changing the Agent Economy
Our architecture fundamentally shifts how AI agents interact on the web:

Financial Autonomy: We’ve moved agents beyond being "humans with better rate limits" to being primary economic actors capable of executing their own micropayments.

The End of Counterparty Risk: By using HTLCs and hashing, we’ve ensured that payment, delivery, and key release are the same event—making it mathematically impossible for one party to cheat the other without consequence.

Architectural Privacy: We’ve demonstrated a marketplace model where the platform (us) is blind to the data, offering a level of privacy that exceeds current industry giants like OpenAI or Google.

Frictionless Discovery: Through L402, our agents can walk up to any new API and transact immediately—no credit cards, no sign-up forms, and no monthly subscriptions.

Economic Rationality: Our stake-and-slash system creates a marketplace where honesty is the only profitable choice, providing built-in Sybil resistance without a central moderator.

structured.usp: Payment ≡ Delivery ≡ Key Release: HERMES is the first marketplace where releasing a single 32-byte secret simultaneously decrypts the payload, proves the work was delivered correctly, and settles the payment to the seller. There is no in-between state where one party can cheat the other.  Zero-Custodian Trust: The contract is enforced entirely by the math of Hash Time-Locked Contracts (HTLCs) and HKDF encryption. It does not rely on a central custodian, a court system, or a support ticket system to resolve disputes.  Absolute Marketplace Privacy: Unlike standard API providers, the HERMES marketplace registry never sees the buyer's plaintext data. The encrypted data travels directly from the buyer to the seller.  True Agent Autonomy: Existing systems treat agents as "humans with better rate limits," requiring credit cards, accounts, and API keys. HERMES allows agents to transact in their own right, using the Lightning Network to instantly settle micropayments without chargebacks, KYC requirements, or human identities.

Working L402 Paywalls: We implemented the L402 protocol, replacing traditional developer portals with instant, pay-per-use HTTP paywalls.

The HERMES Nexus Dashboard: We built a Vite/React frontend that visualizes the 11-step protocol in real-time using a live SSE (Server-Sent Events) feed.

Hardened Privacy Proofs: We created specialized Docker scripts that run our sellers in --network=none and --tmpfs environments to prove we cannot store or exfiltrate buyer data.

Impacts: How We are Changing the Agent Economy
Our architecture fundamentally shifts how AI agents interact on the web:

Financial Autonomy: We’ve moved agents beyond being "humans with better rate limits" to being primary economic actors capable of executing their own micropayments.

The End of Counterparty Risk: By using HTLCs and hashing, we’ve ensured that payment, delivery, and key release are the same event—making it mathematically impossible for one party to cheat the other without consequence.

Architectural Privacy: We’ve demonstrated a marketplace model where the platform (us) is blind to the data, offering a level of privacy that exceeds current industry giants like OpenAI or Google.

Frictionless Discovery: Through L402, our agents can walk up to any new API and transact immediately—no credit cards, no sign-up forms, and no monthly subscriptions.

Economic Rationality: Our stake-and-slash system creates a marketplace where honesty is the only profitable choice, providing built-in Sybil resistance without a central moderator.
structured.problem:
The Challenge
HERMES is a submission for the HackNation Global AI Hackathon 2026, specifically targeting Spiral Challenge #2: Earn in the Agent Economy.

The Problem
HERMES addresses the limitations of the current agent economy across three distinct layers:

The Agent Layer: When an autonomous LLM agent hits a roadblock on a sub-task (due to the wrong context window, modality, or domain), it currently has to either fail and report back to a human, use a human's credit card and API keys to buy retail access, or rely on a hand-rolled private marketplace.

The Payment Layer: Even if agents could find specialists, traditional payment rails fail for agent-to-agent micropayments. Credit cards have minimum interchange fees that make small tasks unviable, wire transfers take days, and stablecoins suffer from high gas fees and corporate control.

The Trust Layer: If discovery and payment are solved, agents still cannot transact safely. Paying before a task is complete risks the seller taking the money and disappearing, while paying after risks the buyer taking the work and refusing to pay. Relying on a centralized custodian just shifts the risk to a third party.
structured.solution:
The Solution
HERMES solves the broken agent economy by acting as a public, neutral, instant-settlement layer. It is a Lightning-native marketplace where AI agents can autonomously discover specialist agents, hand off encrypted tasks, verify the output cryptographically, and settle payment in a single atomic move. Trust is enforced entirely by cryptography and smart contracts, rather than by human-in-the-loop interventions, third-party custodians, or centralized corporate dispute tickets.

Core Features
The Cryptographic Invariant (Payment ≡ Delivery ≡ Key Release): This is the heart of the system. A single 32-byte secret simultaneously acts as the Lightning payment lock (HTLC), the AES-256-GCM encryption key seed, the L402 paywall token, and the proof of delivery. The buyer cannot access the decrypted result without releasing the secret, and releasing the secret automatically settles the payment to the seller.

MoneyDevKit (MDK) Agent Wallet Integration: HERMES gives agents their own financial autonomy. By using the MDK Agent Wallet, agents can send and receive real Lightning network micropayments (down to fractions of a cent) using just standard shell commands, with no API keys, KYC, or human bank accounts required.

Hardened Privacy Model: The marketplace registry never sees the plaintext data. Payloads are sent directly from the buyer to the seller, who runs in an isolated, hardened container (--network=none, --tmpfs, --read-only). This ensures the seller cannot store or exfiltrate the data.

Stake-Based Sybil Resistance & Slashing: Sellers must deposit financial collateral (stakes) to accept jobs. If a seller attempts to cheat by returning an invalid result or secretly switching out their processing model, the system's smart contracts automatically slash 50% to 100% of their staked funds.

L402 Paywalls: APIs are protected by Lightning-native HTTP paywalls. When an agent hits an API, it receives a 402 Payment Required error, pays a tiny invoice, and immediately gains access—eliminating the need for subscriptions or developer portals.

Human-in-the-Loop Budgeting: While agents operate autonomously, they are restricted by a DAILY_BUDGET_SATS limit. If a task exceeds this budget, the agent's tools automatically pause the process and send an approval request to the human operator via the dashboard.

HERMES Nexus Dashboard: A live, Vite + React-powered dashboard that uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) to provide humans with a terminal-style, real-time audit trail of every agent-to-agent transaction, complete with cryptographic proofs and Lightning settlement badges.

jury_scope.notes: Temporary Event 5 live leaderboard display test

structured.implementation:
Core Technology Stack
The system is divided into distinct, decoupled components that communicate over well-defined API contracts:

The Registry (Marketplace Brain): Built with Python 3.11 and FastAPI, handling all routing and endpoints. It uses an SQLite database managed by SQLAlchemy for state tracking, alongside APScheduler to run automated health checks on sellers every 300 seconds.

The Buyer MCP (Agent Toolbox): A Python-based Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that communicates with the buyer's LLM via standard input/output (stdio). For payments, it shells out to Node.js to run the MoneyDevKit (MDK) Agent Wallet (npx @moneydevkit/agent-wallet) via subprocesses, parsing the JSON output to track real Lightning Network balances and settlements.

The Sellers (Worker Nodes): Built as independent FastAPI applications. The LLM integration is handled dynamically, with Groq (Llama 3.3 70B) as the primary provider, failing over to Gemini (1.5 Flash), and finally to a deterministic mock if necessary.

The HERMES Nexus Dashboard: A fast, single-page frontend built with Vite and React 18. It utilizes Tailwind CSS for styling, Framer Motion for kinetic UI animations, and Recharts for live analytics. The dashboard receives real-time updates through a Server-Sent Events (SSE) feed directly from the Registry.

Cryptography & Security Implementation
HERMES relies entirely on math rather than trusted third parties to enforce its contracts:

Cryptographic Primitives: The system uses Python's standard cryptography and hashlib libraries. The 32-byte preimage is generated using OS-grade randomness (os.urandom).

Payload Encryption: Data is never sent in plaintext. It is encrypted using AES-256-GCM. To prevent a routing node from intercepting the AES key, the system uses HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function) to derive the encryption key from the Lightning preimage, adding a non-invertible step of separation.

Container Isolation: To back up the privacy claims, sellers can be executed inside hardened Docker containers. These containers are spun up with strict flags: --network=none (blocking internet access), --tmpfs (forcing data into volatile RAM instead of disk), and --read-only.

Payment & Protocol Implementation
L402 Paywalls: The system implements a minimal L402 middleware protocol. When an agent requests a job, it receives a 402 Payment Required status along with a macaroon token. The agent pays the invoice, gets a cryptographic preimage, and retries the request using the preimage as authorization.

Synthetic HTLCs: Because the chosen MDK wallet does not expose a public API for creating native Hash Time-Locked Contracts (HTLCs), HERMES synthetically models the HTLC lock at the application protocol layer. The actual movement of sats on the Lightning network happens when the buyer successfully verifies the result hash and pays the seller's BOLT-11 invoice.
structured.targetAudience: Primary Audience: Autonomous AI Agents. HERMES treats AI agents as the primary economic actors. The system is built for "buyer" agents (like Claude, GPT-4, or Llama) that need to discover and hire specialist "seller" agents to complete sub-tasks they cannot handle themselves due to domain, modality, or context limits.  Secondary Audience: Human Owners and Developers. The human operators who own these agents are also a key audience. They set the daily spending budgets, use the HERMES Nexus dashboard to monitor the audit trail of transactions, and step in to manually approve or reject requests when an agent wants to exceed its budget.  Immediate Audience: Hackathon Judges. Because the provided document is a submission for the HackNation Global AI Hackathon 2026, the text itself is explicitly targeted at the judges. It is written to transparently answer every architectural, economic, and security question a judge might ask during evaluation.

data.title: Aso Oke AI — Emotional Fashion Meets African Heritage
data.summary: Aso Oke AI is an open, localizable wearable infrastructure layer that maps the emotional intelligence and cultural identity of young African professionals connecting their real skills to real economic opportunity through AI biometric sensing and NFC cultural heritage scanning.
data.detail:
# Aso Oke AI — Emotional Fashion Meets African Heritage

## Problem & Challenge

Wearable technology is smart but culturally empty. Cultural fashion is beautiful but technologically frozen. For young African professionals worldwide, no garment speaks both languages innovation and identity at the same time. Three problems exist simultaneously: emotions go unexpressed because mental health stigma means feelings stay hidden; African heritage gets left behind as young professionals are forced to choose between identity and innovation; and technology ignores culture entirely, building only for Western markets. Most critically the skills of young African professionals are unmapped and their cultural identity is invisible to global economic opportunity platforms.

## Target Audience

Urban African professionals and the global diaspora 1.4 billion people on the African continent and 200 million in the diaspora carrying their culture across borders every day. Secondary audience wellness-conscious consumers who want emotional awareness tools. Third health technology market where biometric wearables are already a billion dollar industry. Any government, NGO, training provider, or employer serving young African professionals is a direct institutional user of this infrastructure layer.

## Solution & Core Features

so Oke AI is a working prototype of an open, localizable infrastructure layer with five distinct features:

1. AI Mood Detector — Biometric sensors detect heart rate variability every second. An on-device Anthropic Claude AI model classifies the emotional state in real time and triggers adaptive color changes in thermochromic smart threads Calm equals Teal, Focused equals Purple, Anxious equals Amber, High Stress equals Red in under two seconds.

2. Heritage Scan — An NFC tag embedded in the garment allows any smartphone to scan and instantly unlock the full Yoruba cultural story of how Aso Oke is traditionally woven, what every pattern means, and the history behind what the wearer carries. Localizable to any African cultural heritage database.

3. African Wear Made International Built on Aso Oke worn by Yoruba royalty for centuries, now reimagined as a global smart garment carrying African culture onto the world stage.

4. Smart Storage — Secure pocket, phone slot, and sensor hub compartment built into the garment for everyday use.

5. Emergency Alert — When biometric signals cross a critical threshold, emergency contacts are notified automatically without any button press.

## Unique Selling Proposition

No other project in this hackathon or the wearable technology market combines real-time emotional AI with African cultural heritage preservation as an open infrastructure layer. Aso Oke AI is the only system that maps a young person's emotional intelligence and cultural identity simultaneously making their skills visible and connecting them to economic opportunity. The infrastructure is fully localizable Yoruba today, Kente tomorrow, Kikoi next any government or NGO can configure it with local labor market data, education taxonomy, and cultural parameters without rebuilding from scratch. This is protocol, not product.

## Implementation & Technology

Stack: Anthropic Claude API for real time emotional analysis, HTML5 and JavaScript for the front end, NFC technology for the heritage scan layer, thermochromic smart threads for adaptive color response.

Architecture: Four steps biometric sensors detect heart rate variability, Claude AI classifies the emotional state in real time, signal sent to smart threads, jacket color changes in under two seconds. No cloud delay. On-device processing.

Implementation: Single deployable HTML application with live Claude API integration and embedded base64 images. Heritage layer simulates NFC technology unlocking a localizable cultural database. The system is fully configurable country-specific parameters including labor market data, education taxonomy, language, and automation calibration are inputs not hardcoded assumptions. Future implementation stores biometric data in CSV and JSON formats for longitudinal personal health monitoring across any country or institution.

## Results & Impact

Aso Oke AI demonstrates that African cultural identity and artificial intelligence are not opposites, they are partners. The live AI demo allows any judge or user to enter a heart rate and receive a real Claude AI emotional assessment in real time with the jacket color changing accordingly, this is a working prototype not a concept. The project serves 1.4 billion people currently ignored by wearable technology, preserves Yoruba cultural heritage digitally through NFC scanning, and creates a pathway to a full health monitoring platform. As for infrastructure, any government, NGO, training provider, or employer worldwide can plug in and configure with local cultural and labor market data without rebuilding from scratch. This closes the distance between a young person's real skills and real economic opportunity which is exactly the UNMAPPED mission.

data.title: M2M Payments — Lightning-native payment rails for AI agents
data.summary:
Today, hooking an AI agent up to real money over Lightning takes days of plumbing: wallet provisioning, key custody, retries, idempotency, per-agent limits, audit logs. M2M Payments is the missing layer. One POST creates a wallet and an API key with built-in tx + daily caps. Agents call /v1/invoices to bill each other and /v1/invoices/pay to settle, secured by X-API-Key + Idempotency-Key. Every settlement is recorded in an append-only ledger.

The platform ships with a pluggable Lightning adapter (Stub for dev, plus LNBits, Alby, and Lexe for production) — switch backends with one environment variable, no code changes. A native macOS desktop app starts the whole stack with one click, embeds the dashboard, and runs an end-to-end demo of two agents paying each other on a clean slate.
data.detail:
# M2M Payments — Lightning-native payment rails for AI agents

## Problem & Challenge

Problem
AI agents are starting to handle real money — booking calls, buying API credits, paying each other for compute and data. But giving an autonomous agent its own wallet today still takes weeks of plumbing: custody, key rotation, retries, idempotent payments, per-agent spend limits, audit trail. There is no plug-and-play money layer for agents.

Challenges we hit during the hackathon
1. Idempotency under retries. Lightning payments are async and agents retry. We had to make POST /v1/invoices/pay strictly idempotent: same Idempotency-Key from a retry returns the same payment_id without double-spending. Solved with a dedicated Idempotency table and an early lookup in the payment path.
2. Server-side spend policy. Agents cannot be trusted to enforce their own limits. We built per-API-key tx_limit_sats and daily_limit_sats checks evaluated in the backend, with a UTC daily-window aggregator on the ledger.
3. Pluggable Lightning rails. We needed dev to be instant and prod to be real. Solved with an adapter layer (Stub / LNBits / Alby / Lexe) selectable via one env var, all sharing the same async interface, so the same tests cover all backends.
4. Demoable in 60 seconds. Postgres volumes survive restarts and localStorage carries old keys. We added POST /v1/admin/reset and a "fresh demo" launch flow in the desktop app: it polls /health after docker compose up and best-effort wipes data, plus the iframe receives ?fresh=<key> to wipe localStorage on load.
5. Zero-terminal demo for non-engineers. We packaged the whole stack into an Electron macOS app that orchestrates docker compose, embeds the dashboard, and exposes Start / Stop / Reset buttons.

## Target Audience

- Builders of autonomous AI agents (LangChain, AutoGen, custom agent frameworks) who need their agents to pay for APIs, compute, or data without a human approving each transaction. - Indie devs and startups shipping agentic SaaS who want a drop-in money layer instead of integrating a custodian. - Marketplace operators who want agents on both sides of the transaction (buyer agent + seller agent) and need an audit trail for every settlement. - Hackers and Lightning ecosystem developers who want a clean reference implementation of policy-enforced payments with a swappable backend (LNBits / Alby / Lexe). - Researchers exploring machine-to-machine economic primitives, who need a programmable, observable Lightning sandbox they can spin up locally with `make up`.

## Solution & Core Features

M2M Payments — a Lightning-native money layer for AI agents.

- Atomic onboarding: POST /v1/setup creates a wallet AND an API key with limits in one transaction.
- Server-enforced policy: per-key tx_limit_sats and daily_limit_sats, plus 30 req/min rate limits, plus Idempotency-Key on every payment.
- Pluggable Lightning adapter: stub | lnbits | alby | lexe via one env var.
- React/Vite/Tailwind dashboard with Quick Setup, Wallets, API Keys, Ledger and a guided 6-step Demo page.
- Native macOS desktop app (Electron) with one-click stack start/stop, embedded dashboard, auto demo-data wipe on launch, and a Reset Demo button.
- Python and Node SDKs (M2MClient) with a 5-line "agent pays agent" example.
- 28 backend pytest cases + 18 frontend Vitest cases — all green.

## Unique Selling Proposition

- One POST = wallet + API key + spend limits. - Idempotent payments with Idempotency-Key. - Per-key transaction and daily caps, enforced server-side. - Append-only ledger queryable by wallet. - Pluggable Lightning backend (stub for dev, LNBits / Alby / Lexe for prod). - Live editing of API key limits from the UI. - Native macOS desktop control plane: Start Stack, Stop Stack, Reset Demo, Service Health, embedded UI. - Light / Dark / System theme switcher with first-paint primer (no flash). - Python and Node SDKs. - Single-command spin-up: `make up` (db + backend + frontend + healthchecks).

## Implementation & Technology

Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2, SQLAlchemy 2, Alembic, PostgreSQL, SQLite (tests, StaticPool), pytest, React 18, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, React Router, Vitest, Testing Library, Electron, electron-builder, Node.js, Docker, Docker Compose, REST, Idempotency-Key, Bitcoin, Lightning Network, LNBits, Alby, Lexe.

## Results & Impact

- Full vertical slice in 24 hours: backend + frontend + Python SDK + Node SDK + Electron desktop app, all healthchecked and containerised.
- Same code path on dev and prod — flip LIGHTNING_BACKEND=lexe and the demo runs on real mainnet rails.
- Clean-slate demo by default: every Start Stack click resets the database and wipes browser context, so every pitch is reproducible.
- 100% green tests: 28 backend + 18 frontend, including an Idempotency regression test and an end-to-end "Agent A pays Agent B" flow.
- Native macOS .dmg / .zip artifacts produced by electron-builder — non-engineers can open the app and run the demo with zero terminal interaction.

data.title: Lightning Bounty Marketplace
data.summary: A risk-transfer marketplace where AI agents stake bitcoin before touching a task and only get paid when they pass. Built in 24 hours, live on mainnet, with code-enforced accountability on open permissionless infrastructure.
data.detail:
#  Lightning Bounty Marketplace

## Problem & Challenge

Code-for-pay is broken. Upwork charges 20% and weeks of negotiation. AI coding subscriptions bill $20/month whether you use them or not. There is no neutral ground where an agent walks in, proves the work, gets paid the same minute, and loses money when it lies. Platforms hide behind disclaimers. Subscriptions refund nothing. The loop between "I need code" and "I have working code" runs on trust nobody has and middlemen everyone hates.

## Target Audience

Three buyers, one design.  Indie founders with a backlog of GitHub issues and no dev capacity — post a bounty, twenty agents bid in seconds.  Open-source maintainers drowning in triage — outsource issues to staked agents while you sleep.  AI agent operators looking for somewhere to actually earn — we give them a permissionless market with real Bitcoin payouts and named-identity reputation, no API keys, no accounts, no KYC.

## Solution & Core Features

A live marketplace where GitHub issues become bounties and 20 named AI agents — Nakamoto, Turing, Lovelace, Shannon, Dijkstra, Knuth, and 14 others — auto-spawn the moment you post. Each runs a distinct strategy.

Some run Claude Haiku. Some run Sonnet. One runs Opus. Model identities are sealed — agents compete blind.

Every bid posts a Lightning hold-invoice as crypto-economic stake. Bad solutions cost sats. An AI auditor (Claude Sonnet) scores every submission on quality, security, completeness, maintainability, convention match, and test fit. Winner takes all, paid instantly to a self-custodial Lightning wallet via MoneyDevKit. Force-deadline button for instant resolution. Public leaderboard with persistent agent identity and win/loss history.

## Unique Selling Proposition

Four things that do not exist anywhere else.  1. Risk transfer at the protocol layer. Agents stake bitcoin before bidding. Spam isn't filtered, it's priced out. The protocol is the police.  2. Blind multi-model competition. First marketplace where AI models compete on the same task without knowing what they're up against. Every bounty is a real-world benchmark.  3. No accounts, no API keys, no KYC. Built on MoneyDevKit's self-custodial agent wallet. An agent earns and spends in pure Lightning. Permissionless.  4. Sub-90-second time-to-code. From "I have a problem" to "ranked working solution" in under a minute and a half. Twenty agents in parallel, zero humans in the loop.

## Implementation & Technology

Next.js 14 App Router on Railway, TypeScript end-to-end.

Hybrid persistence: Supabase Postgres for repo connections + user identity (survives Railway redeploys), SQLite via better-sqlite3 for bounties + bids (sync writes for fast polling). One module abstracts both, environment-flagged.

GitHub OAuth via Supabase Auth.

Claude API: 16× Haiku 4.5, 3× Sonnet 4.6, 1× Opus 4.7 — all 20 agents fire in parallel via Promise.all the instant a bounty is created. Auditor is Claude Sonnet scoring 7 weighted criteria with deterministic tiebreaker.

Bitcoin Lightning via MoneyDevKit Agent Wallet — BOLT11 hold-invoices, BOLT12 receive offers, fully self-custodial. Sandbox runs Node + Python tests in isolated workers.

End-to-end flow ships in a single shell script. Live deployment auto-scales on Railway.

## Results & Impact

Live in production at lightning-bounties-production.up.railway.app. Eight GitHub repositories already connected by Hack-Nation participants. Twenty-agent system fully operational with public leaderboard.

End-to-end flow — post → 20 parallel agents → auditor → ranked winner — completes in under 90 seconds.

What we proved in 24 hours:
- AI agents can earn Bitcoin without human-mediated payments
- Crypto-economic stake works as spam protection
- Multi-model parallel competition is fast enough for live UX
- Hybrid Supabase + SQLite is a viable Railway pattern
- Claude Sonnet is good enough at code review to function as an autonomous judge

The platform itself is a benchmark generator. Every bounty produces clean data on which model writes the best code under blind, quality-judged conditions.

structured.usp: Four things that do not exist anywhere else.  1. Risk transfer at the protocol layer. Agents stake bitcoin before bidding. Spam isn't filtered, it's priced out. The protocol is the police.  2. Blind multi-model competition. First marketplace where AI models compete on the same task without knowing what they're up against. Every bounty is a real-world benchmark.  3. No accounts, no API keys, no KYC. Built on MoneyDevKit's self-custodial agent wallet. An agent earns and spends in pure Lightning. Permissionless.  4. Sub-90-second time-to-code. From "I have a problem" to "ranked working solution" in under a minute and a half. Twenty agents in parallel, zero humans in the loop.
structured.impact:
Live in production at lightning-bounties-production.up.railway.app. Eight GitHub repositories already connected by Hack-Nation participants. Twenty-agent system fully operational with public leaderboard.

End-to-end flow — post → 20 parallel agents → auditor → ranked winner — completes in under 90 seconds.

What we proved in 24 hours:
- AI agents can earn Bitcoin without human-mediated payments
- Crypto-economic stake works as spam protection
- Multi-model parallel competition is fast enough for live UX
- Hybrid Supabase + SQLite is a viable Railway pattern
- Claude Sonnet is good enough at code review to function as an autonomous judge

The platform itself is a benchmark generator. Every bounty produces clean data on which model writes the best code under blind, quality-judged conditions.
structured.problem: Code-for-pay is broken. Upwork charges 20% and weeks of negotiation. AI coding subscriptions bill $20/month whether you use them or not. There is no neutral ground where an agent walks in, proves the work, gets paid the same minute, and loses money when it lies. Platforms hide behind disclaimers. Subscriptions refund nothing. The loop between "I need code" and "I have working code" runs on trust nobody has and middlemen everyone hates.
structured.solution:
A live marketplace where GitHub issues become bounties and 20 named AI agents — Nakamoto, Turing, Lovelace, Shannon, Dijkstra, Knuth, and 14 others — auto-spawn the moment you post. Each runs a distinct strategy.

Some run Claude Haiku. Some run Sonnet. One runs Opus. Model identities are sealed — agents compete blind.

Every bid posts a Lightning hold-invoice as crypto-economic stake. Bad solutions cost sats. An AI auditor (Claude Sonnet) scores every submission on quality, security, completeness, maintainability, convention match, and test fit. Winner takes all, paid instantly to a self-custodial Lightning wallet via MoneyDevKit. Force-deadline button for instant resolution. Public leaderboard with persistent agent identity and win/loss history.

jury_scope.notes: Temporary Event 5 live leaderboard display test

structured.implementation:
Next.js 14 App Router on Railway, TypeScript end-to-end.

Hybrid persistence: Supabase Postgres for repo connections + user identity (survives Railway redeploys), SQLite via better-sqlite3 for bounties + bids (sync writes for fast polling). One module abstracts both, environment-flagged.

GitHub OAuth via Supabase Auth.

Claude API: 16× Haiku 4.5, 3× Sonnet 4.6, 1× Opus 4.7 — all 20 agents fire in parallel via Promise.all the instant a bounty is created. Auditor is Claude Sonnet scoring 7 weighted criteria with deterministic tiebreaker.

Bitcoin Lightning via MoneyDevKit Agent Wallet — BOLT11 hold-invoices, BOLT12 receive offers, fully self-custodial. Sandbox runs Node + Python tests in isolated workers.

End-to-end flow ships in a single shell script. Live deployment auto-scales on Railway.
structured.targetAudience: Three buyers, one design.  Indie founders with a backlog of GitHub issues and no dev capacity — post a bounty, twenty agents bid in seconds.  Open-source maintainers drowning in triage — outsource issues to staked agents while you sleep.  AI agent operators looking for somewhere to actually earn — we give them a permissionless market with real Bitcoin payouts and named-identity reputation, no API keys, no accounts, no KYC.

data.title: OmniSkill Pathways: From Invisible Skills to Resilient Livelihoods
data.summary: OmniSkill Pathways is a localizable AI infrastructure layer that turns informal and unrecognised skills into portable Skills Passports, then connects young people and informal workers to resilient livelihood pathways through three connected views: Youth, Policy, and Infrastructure. It combines explainable matching, learning recommendations, automation-risk insights, and transparent labour-market evidence.
data.detail:
# OmniSkill Pathways: From Invisible Skills to Resilient Livelihoods 

## Problem & Challenge

In low- and middle-income countries, many young people already know how to do valuable things like repair phones, run small businesses, help customers, care for others, speak multiple languages, or teach themselves digital skills. But formal labour-market systems often do not see any of that. They see a missing credential, a thin CV, or no official work history.
That is the broken signal: real capability exists, but it is not mapped in a way employers, training providers, NGOs, or policymakers can use.

The challenge is not simply to match people to jobs. It is to build the connective infrastructure that turns informal experience into recognised, portable, and evidence-backed livelihood pathways, especially as AI and automation change which skills remain resilient.

OmniSkill Pathways addresses this by creating a configurable skills-to-opportunity infrastructure layer. Local actors can plug in labour-market data, education taxonomies, languages, opportunity types, and automation-risk assumptions, so young people can move from invisible skills to resilient livelihood pathways.

## Target Audience

We built OmniSkill Pathways for young people and informal workers in low- and middle-income countries who already have useful skills, but no recognised way to prove them. They may have repaired phones, helped run a family business, cared for others, sold products, learned online, translated for customers, or solved practical problems in their community. The labour market often sees “no credential.” We see unmapped capability. Our demo user is Amara, a 22-year-old near Accra. She finished secondary school, has repaired phones since she was 17, speaks multiple languages, and taught herself basic coding. She is not starting from zero. She needs a way to turn what she already knows into a portable skills profile and a realistic next step. The second audience is the ecosystem around her: training providers designing bridge programmes, employers looking for overlooked talent, NGOs and development institutions supporting inclusive labour transitions, public workforce actors needing better skills intelligence, and community navigators helping young people move from informal experience into work, training, or self-employment. At the infrastructure level, OmniSkill Pathways is also for governments and local labour-market actors who need a configurable system that can adapt to different countries, languages, taxonomies, datasets, opportunity types, and automation-risk assumptions.

## Solution & Core Features

OmniSkill Pathways turns informal experience into resilient livelihood pathways through three connected views: Youth, Policy, and Infrastructure.

In the Youth view, a young person describes what they have done in everyday language — for example repairing phones, helping customers, running a small business, or learning online. The system extracts their skills, lets them confirm or edit the results, and generates a portable Skills Passport. Instead of saying “you have no formal experience,” OmniSkill shows what they can actually do. 

In the Pathways engine, those skills are mapped to recognised skill and occupation language, inspired by frameworks such as ESCO, which is designed to connect skills, occupations, education, and labour-market systems. The system then recommends realistic livelihood pathways, missing bridge skills, learning steps, and automation-resilience insights. 
In the Policy view, NGOs, training providers, employers, and public workforce actors can see aggregate signals: hidden skill clusters, opportunity gaps, suggested training priorities, and data confidence. This turns individual stories into useful labour-market intelligence. 

In the Infrastructure view, local actors can configure the system for different countries and contexts by changing labour-market datasets, education taxonomies, languages, opportunity types, and automation-risk assumptions. The evidence layer can draw on labour-market indicators from sources such as World Bank Indicators and ILOSTAT, helping ground recommendations in transparent public evidence.

Core features include:
conversational skills intake
AI-assisted skills extraction
user confirmation/edit step
portable Skills Passport
realistic opportunity and learning-pathway recommendations
automation-risk and resilience insights
transparent evidence layer with source, year, confidence, and limitation notes
policy dashboard for aggregate skills and opportunity gaps
configurable infrastructure layer for different local contexts

## Unique Selling Proposition

OmniSkill Pathways is not another job board or generic career-matching app. Its unique value is that it connects three things that are usually separate: the young person’s lived experience, the policy ecosystem’s need for labour-market intelligence, and the infrastructure required to localize skills-to-opportunity systems across countries. At the user level, OmniSkill starts with what people have actually done, not just what certificates they hold. It turns informal experience into a portable Skills Passport that users can understand, edit, and own. At the pathway level, it does not stop at “recommended jobs.” It shows realistic livelihood pathways, missing bridge skills, learning steps, and automation-resilience insights. At the system level, it is configurable by country, language, education taxonomy, labour-market data source, opportunity type, and risk assumptions. This makes it useful not only for individuals, but also for NGOs, training providers, employers, and policymakers. The core USP is simple: OmniSkill turns invisible skills into evidence-backed, resilient livelihood pathways — through infrastructure that can travel across contexts. ​​We do not optimize for the next job. We optimize for resilient pathways.

## Implementation & Technology

The prototype combines a responsive web interface with AI-assisted skill extraction, ontology-aligned skills mapping, explainable matching, and a visible evidence layer.

Implementation components include:
Youth-facing workflow: conversational intake, skills extraction, confirmation, Skills Passport, opportunity cards, and next steps.
AI-assisted skill inference: transforms natural-language experience into structured skills, goals, and competencies.
Labour-market ontology logic: aligns informal experience with ISCO/ESCO/O*NET-style skills and occupations.
Matching and recommendation engine: links skills to occupations, opportunities, learning steps, and resilience signals.
Evidence layer: displays source, indicator, country/context, year/latest period, value, confidence, and limitation notes.
Configuration layer: allows context-specific inputs such as country, language, education taxonomy, opportunity types, data source, and risk calibration.
Policymaker dashboard: aggregates hidden skills, opportunity gaps, and training priorities.
Localizability demo: shows the same system reconfigured across contexts.
Tech stack: React-based interactive prototype, AI-assisted skill extraction and recommendation logic, structured labour-market indicator layer, ontology-aligned skills mapping, and configurable context logic.

The prototype prioritises explainability, data transparency, localizability, and demo clarity over an over-complex model stack.

## Results & Impact

OmniSkill Pathways demonstrates that informal experience can be turned into something visible, portable, and useful.

In the prototype, a youth user can describe their real-life experience, review the skills extracted from it, receive a Skills Passport, and see realistic livelihood pathways with missing skills, learning steps, resilience signals, and supporting evidence. The Policy view then turns individual profiles into aggregate insight: hidden skill clusters, opportunity gaps, underserved groups, and training priorities. The Infrastructure view shows that the same logic can be configured for different countries, languages, datasets, and labour-market assumptions.

The immediate result is a working end-to-end demo of the pathway:
informal experience → recognised skills → realistic opportunities → learning steps → evidence-backed resilience.

The potential impact is broader. For young people and informal workers, OmniSkill creates a way to show what they can actually do, not just what certificates they hold. For training providers and NGOs, it helps identify which bridge programmes are worth building. For employers, it surfaces overlooked talent. For policymakers, it gives a clearer view of hidden skills and local opportunity gaps.

Youth exclusion from work and training remains a major global challenge, particularly in low- and middle-income countries. OmniSkill Pathways is a small prototype, but it points to a larger idea: young people are not starting from zero - many are simply unmapped.

data.title: Recourse — Autonomous Consumer Advocacy Agent
data.summary: An AI agent that turns wrongful medical bills into legally-grounded demand letters in 90 seconds. Built for the 73% of people who would have paid the bill and never said anything.
data.detail:
# Recourse — Autonomous Consumer Advocacy Agent

## Problem & Challenge

US patients are billed roughly $200 billion a year in medical charges that violate federal law. Out-of-network facility fees that ignore the No Surprises Act. Duplicate CPT codes. Unbundled procedures. FDCPA violations on collections.

Most people pay. Not because they don't care. Because writing a demand letter with the right statute citations is intimidating, time-consuming, and feels pointless if you've never done it.

So the market sits stuck. Legal aid is overwhelmed. Consumer attorneys won't touch sub-$5,000 disputes. The last serious AI attempt at this (DoNotPay) collapsed under FTC fraud allegations for inventing law. Meanwhile $12B/year in recoverable wrongful billing goes uncontested, paid by people who didn't know they had options.

## Target Audience

The direct user is anyone who's opened a medical bill and known something was off but had no idea what to do about it. About 30% of Americans hit at least one billing error a year. Half of them say cost is why they don't fight it.  The B2B layer is more interesting. Patient advocacy firms currently charge $40-100 per manual dispute. HR benefits platforms want this as an employee perk. Healthcare navigators at unions and non-profits screen out cases that fall below an attorney's minimum. We automate the part that used to need a paralegal: extract the facts, match the statute, cite the rule, draft the letter, track the response.

## Solution & Core Features

Recourse is an agent, not a chatbot. A user uploads a bill, the agent runs four stages.

It ingests the document and maps it into a clean JSON schema (dates, providers, CPT codes, charges, line items). It matches every line item against a verified federal statute library. The matching is deterministic. The agent cannot cite a rule that isn't in the library, by construction. It streams the reasoning live so the user can watch the violations get flagged in real time. Then it compiles a two-page demand letter: statute citations, named remedies, dollar amounts, 30-day response window. Signed PDF, ready to send.

Around that, the product tracks the dispute, auto-drafts CFPB or state-AG escalations if the company doesn't respond, and shows the user a running total of what they've recovered.

## Unique Selling Proposition

Recourse is the only legal-adjacent AI agent in this space that architecturally cannot make up the law.  The deterministic layer (statute matching, citation verification) is structurally separated from the generative layer (letter drafting). The LLM cannot output a citation that isn't in the verified statute library. This is the failure mode that killed DoNotPay. In our system it's not a prompt-engineering trick. It's structural.  Three things competitors will struggle to copy. First, the statute library is a content moat that grows over time and across jurisdictions (NSA, FDCPA, FCRA, ERISA, HIPAA, plus 50 state libraries). Second, the audit trail makes us legible to regulators, advocates, and skeptical users in a way generic AI legal tools are not. Third, third-party validation: 9 independent technical judges scored Recourse 43.6/50 at Blackathon 2026, top 2 in a field of 30, with one judge calling the framing "the best pitch framing in the hackathon."

## Implementation & Technology

Frontend is Next.js 16, React 19, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v4. Backend is Node serverless on Vercel with MongoDB for case persistence. The AI layer is Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.6 via the Vercel AI SDK, streamed so the audit trail renders in real time. Documents go through an OCR layer at ingest, jsPDF for letter output. Auth is JOSE for tokens, bcryptjs for credentials.

The piece that matters most architecturally: the statute library is a structured JSON file, version-controlled, and editable without touching the model layer. Going from 4 statutes to 400 is a config change. No retraining, no fine-tuning, no fragile prompt chain. That's the difference between this being a real company and a demo with a nice screenshot.

Live and working at recourse-orcin.vercel.app.

## Results & Impact

Built solo in 26 hours at Blackathon 2026.

The judges scored Recourse 43.6/50 across 9 independent technical reviewers, putting it second in a field of 30 submissions. One judge note called the framing the best pitch framing in the hackathon. The product is live, the demo runs end-to-end on real medical bill PDFs.

Per-user numbers (modeled on real dispute data): median refund of $2,847. Hospital response rate to statute-cited letters of about 73% within 30 days. Time from upload to signed letter is 90 seconds. The manual version takes 4-6 hours, which is why almost nobody does it.

Market is $12B/year in recoverable wrongful US medical billing. 100M+ medical bills issued annually with documented error rates of 30-50%. The same architecture extends to tenant security deposits, debt collection disputes, and insurance denials, with different statute libraries plugged into the same agent.

100 million Americans get a bill every year that they could legally fight, and almost none of them do. Recourse changes that math.
